_meta.authoringNotes: Numbers cross-checked against Hirst 2008 surgical outcomes review (PMID 18929129), Coroneo 1993 pathogenesis hypothesis, AAO Preferred Practice Pattern: Conjunctivitis/Cornea, Cochrane review on conjunctival autograft, and WHO UV exposure epidemiology. Surgical recurrence figures verified against Kenyon 1985, Clearfield 2016 Cochrane, and MMC adjunct meta-analyses.

h1Title: Pterygium (Surfer's Eye): Symptoms, Causes, Surgery, and Recurrence
heroOverview: Pterygium is a wing-shaped fibrovascular overgrowth of the conjunctiva that creeps across the limbus onto the cornea, driven primarily by years of ultraviolet light exposure. The common name is Surfer's Eye, but the condition affects anyone who spends time outdoors near the equator, on water, on snow, or in dusty windy environments. Prevalence reaches 10-15% within 37 degrees of the equator and drops below 3% in temperate latitudes. Most pterygia grow slowly and need only lubricants and UV protection, but a fleshy, vascularized lesion that approaches the visual axis induces with-the-rule astigmatism, blurs vision, and eventually warrants surgical removal. Modern conjunctival autograft surgery keeps recurrence below 10% in experienced hands.

keyStats.prevalence: 10-15% in equatorial belt populations; 1-3% in temperate climates
keyStats.demographics: Outdoor workers and men aged 20-50 most affected; 10x higher within 37 degrees of equator
keyStats.avgAge: First diagnosed between ages 30 and 50; childhood pterygium is rare and suggests xeroderma pigmentosum
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 100-150 million people worldwide; the leading conjunctival surgical pathology in tropical countries

definition: Pterygium of eye (ICD-10: H11.00) is a benign, slowly progressive degenerative and proliferative disorder of the bulbar conjunctiva in which a triangular fibrovascular tissue invades the cornea, typically from the nasal side. The lesion is named from the Greek pterygion meaning little wing, and its body lies on the sclera while its apex or head advances across the limbus onto Bowman layer of the cornea. Histologically, pterygium combines elastotic degeneration of the substantia propria with fibroblastic proliferation, neovascularization, inflammatory infiltrate, and dissolution of Bowman membrane at the leading edge. The Coroneo hypothesis explains the nasal predominance: peripheral light entering temporally is focused by the cornea onto the nasal limbus, concentrating ultraviolet B radiation on the limbal stem cell barrier and producing local stem cell failure that allows conjunctival migration onto the cornea. Pterygium is distinct from pinguecula, a yellowish elevation that does not cross the limbus, and from ocular surface squamous neoplasia, which can mimic an inflamed pterygium clinically. Management runs from observation with topical lubricants to conjunctival autograft excision when the lesion threatens vision, induces significant astigmatism, or fails to respond to medical therapy. The condition is managed by ophthalmologists, with cornea subspecialists handling recurrent and advanced cases.

typesClassification.description: Thin, translucent, minimally vascular lesion with episcleral vessels clearly visible through the body. Low risk of recurrence after excision (under 5% in published series).
Episcleral vessels partially obscured. Moderate vascularity and thickness; intermediate recurrence risk after surgery.
Thick, opaque, highly vascular lesion that completely hides episcleral vessels. Highest recurrence rate after surgery; adjunctive mitomycin C or amniotic membrane is often used.
First occurrence in an eye that has not had previous pterygium surgery. Standard conjunctival autograft is the gold-standard treatment.
Regrowth after prior surgical excision, usually within 12 months. Often more aggressive, fibrotic, and may involve symblepharon (lid-globe adhesion). Requires advanced surgical planning.
Lesions on both the nasal and temporal sides of the same eye. More common in chronically exposed outdoor workers and may require staged surgery.

primarySymptoms: Visible wing-shaped or triangular fleshy growth on the white of the eye, most often extending from the nasal corner toward the cornea, present in roughly 85-90% of cases.
Persistent foreign body sensation, often described as feeling like a grain of sand under the lid, which worsens in wind, dust, and dry indoor heating.
Recurrent redness over the lesion, fluctuating with environmental exposure and tear film instability rather than infection.
Burning, itching, and tearing of the affected eye, often misdiagnosed and treated as allergic conjunctivitis for months before the pterygium is recognized.
Blurred or fluctuating vision once the head of the pterygium induces astigmatism, typically when 2.5-3 mm or closer to the visual axis.
With-the-rule astigmatism on refraction, frequently 0.5 to 3.0 diopters of induced cylinder, that does not fully correct with glasses if irregular.
Cosmetic concern about a permanent red or pink fleshy mass on the eye, especially in younger patients and those in service-facing occupations.
Inflammatory flares with hyperemia, mild discomfort, and increased thickness of the lesion lasting days to weeks, often triggered by sun, smoke, or contact lens wear.
Reduced contrast and glare sensitivity, particularly at night and in bright sun, due to surface irregularity at the cornea-pterygium interface.
In advanced cases, restriction of eye movement when the pterygium becomes large enough to mechanically tether the conjunctiva to the cornea.
earlyWarningSigns: A small triangle of thickened pink tissue at the inner corner of the eye that does not resolve with two weeks of lubricating drops
Persistent foreign body sensation in one eye after years of outdoor work, surfing, fishing, or farming without UV-protective eyewear
Slowly increasing astigmatism on annual eye exams in an adult who previously had stable refraction
Mild visible blood vessels invading the cornea from the limbus, even without an obvious fleshy body
emergencySigns: Rapid growth of a conjunctival mass over weeks rather than months, particularly with keratin (leukoplakia), atypical vasculature, or papillary surface — raises suspicion for ocular surface squamous neoplasia
Severe pain, photophobia, and reduced vision in a previously stable pterygium — may indicate corneal melt, perforation, or scleral necrosis (especially after mitomycin-C exposure)
Sudden change in eye position, double vision, or restricted eye movement — suggests symblepharon, scarring, or rectus muscle involvement requiring urgent cornea referral
Acute redness, purulent discharge, and severe pain in a postoperative eye — possible infectious scleritis or endophthalmitis after pterygium surgery

causes.description: UV-B radiation between 280 and 315 nm is the dominant driver. It damages limbal stem cells, induces p53 mutations, and triggers elastotic degeneration of the conjunctival stroma. Lifetime UV-B dose correlates strongly with pterygium incidence.

Solar irradiance and reflected UV are highest within 37 degrees of the equator and at altitude. Prevalence is 10-15% in those zones versus 1-3% in temperate latitudes, even for the same occupational profile.
Chronic ocular surface desiccation and micro-trauma from airborne particulates (Mongolian dust, Saharan sand, desert work) accelerate conjunctival fibrovascular proliferation independent of UV.

Farmers, fishermen, surfers, sailors, construction workers, miners, welders, and military personnel accumulate the highest UV doses. Time outdoors before age 16 carries the strongest risk signal in case-control studies.

The limbus normally acts as a barrier preventing conjunctival cells from crossing onto the cornea. Chronic UV injury depletes limbal stem cells, allowing conjunctival migration and the characteristic wing-shaped invasion.

Familial clustering exists; HLA-DR53 and certain matrix metalloproteinase variants are over-represented. p53 mutations are found in roughly 40% of pterygium tissue, suggesting a UV-induced mutational driver shared with sun-related skin neoplasia.

causes.cause: Dry eye and ocular surface inflammation
causes.description: Reduced tear film stability and increased osmolarity create a permissive inflammatory milieu. Patients with chronic dry eye or meibomian gland dysfunction develop pterygia more readily under equivalent UV exposure.

riskFactors.factor: Living within 37 degrees of the equator
riskFactors.description: Solar UV-B at the surface is 5-10 times higher in tropical latitudes than at 50 degrees latitude. Prevalence in equatorial belt populations reaches 10-15%.
Cumulative outdoor exposure above 6 hours daily roughly triples pterygium risk. Welders and people exposed to reflected UV from water, snow, or sand are at the high end of this range.
Men are affected 2-4 times more often than women in most case series, primarily because of occupational exposure patterns rather than biological difference.
Onset peaks in young to middle-aged adults with sufficient cumulative UV exposure but still active growth biology. Childhood pterygium is rare and should prompt evaluation for xeroderma pigmentosum.

riskFactors.factor: Light iris color and fair skin
riskFactors.description: Reduced ocular and skin pigment provides less natural UV filtering, increasing limbal photo-damage. Fitzpatrick I-II skin types carry a modestly elevated risk.
Wraparound polarized sunglasses block roughly 96% of UV-B reaching the cornea. Consistent use from childhood is the single most effective preventive measure.

riskFactors.factor: Chronic dry eye and ocular surface disease
riskFactors.description: Tear film instability, meibomian gland dysfunction, and chronic surface inflammation create the local environment in which conjunctival cells proliferate and migrate.
First-degree relatives of patients have a 2-3 fold increased risk after adjusting for shared environment. p53 and matrix metalloproteinase variants have been implicated.

Tobacco smoke is paradoxically associated with a small reduction in pterygium incidence in some studies, but it worsens ocular surface inflammation, dry eye, and postoperative healing — making it net harmful for affected patients.

riskFactors.factor: Exposure to airborne particulates (dust, sand, welding fumes)
riskFactors.description: Mongolian and Saharan dust regions show pterygium rates above the latitude-predicted baseline. Chronic micro-trauma to the conjunctiva is the proposed mechanism.

affectedDemographics: Highest prevalence in Australia, Pacific Islands, Southeast Asia, Sub-Saharan Africa, the Middle East, and equatorial Latin America
Australian Indigenous populations show rates exceeding 20% in some communities of outdoor workers
Surfers and fishermen in tropical regions carry the highest occupational risk; cumulative dose before age 16 is the strongest predictor
Children are rarely affected; pediatric pterygium should prompt evaluation for xeroderma pigmentosum or other photosensitivity syndromes
US prevalence is roughly 2-3% nationally but exceeds 10% in Hawaii, Florida Keys, and the US-Mexico border regions
diagnosisOverview: Pterygium is a clinical diagnosis made at the slit lamp. The examiner identifies a triangular fibrovascular tissue with its body on the bulbar conjunctiva and its apex (head) crossing the limbus onto the cornea, typically nasally. Grading is by the Tan classification (T1 atrophic, T2 intermediate, T3 fleshy) based on how visible the underlying episcleral vessels are through the lesion; T3 pterygia carry the highest postoperative recurrence risk and influence the choice of adjunctive amniotic membrane or mitomycin C. Distance from the visual axis is measured directly with the slit-lamp reticule, and any lesion within 3 mm of the pupil center is considered visually threatening. Corneal topography (Pentacam, OPD-Scan, or similar) quantifies induced with-the-rule astigmatism; cylinder above 1.0 diopter or irregular astigmatism that does not correct with glasses is a standard surgical indication. Slit-lamp photography establishes a growth baseline so that progression at follow-up visits is objective rather than impressionistic. Anterior segment OCT can map the depth and limbal involvement of recurrent or atypical lesions. Excisional biopsy with histopathology is reserved for atypical presentations — rapid growth, leukoplakia, irregular surface, atypical vessels, or unilateral disease in a non-classic location — to exclude ocular surface squamous neoplasia (OSSN), amyloid deposition, conjunctival lymphoma, or atypical infection. Tear film and meibomian gland evaluation is part of the workup, because untreated dry eye worsens surgical outcomes and accelerates recurrence. Differential diagnosis includes pinguecula (does not cross the limbus), conjunctival cyst, OSSN, conjunctival nevus, and chronic allergic conjunctivitis with papillary hyperplasia.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Primary clinical diagnostic tool. Confirms the wing-shaped fibrovascular lesion crossing the limbus, grades vascularity (T1-T3), and measures distance from the visual axis to plan surgical timing.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An ophthalmologist examines the eye with a slit-lamp microscope after instilling a drop of fluorescein. The exam takes 5-10 minutes and is painless. Bright light may cause brief photophobia.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies induced astigmatism and irregularity caused by the pterygium. Cylinder over 1.0 diopter or irregular astigmatism is a recognized surgical indication and helps predict postoperative refractive outcomes.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Non-contact scan lasting under one minute per eye. The patient fixates on a target while the device captures thousands of corneal data points. No radiation or eye contact.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Establishes objective baseline size, vascularity, and distance to visual axis so that progression can be tracked at 3-6 month intervals.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standardized photographs taken through the slit-lamp or with a dedicated anterior segment camera. Painless and adds 2-3 minutes to a routine visit.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Maps the depth, limbal involvement, and stromal extension of the pterygium. Particularly useful in recurrent disease and to plan the depth of lamellar dissection.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Non-contact infrared scan lasting under 30 seconds per eye. Comfortable for almost all patients and produces cross-sectional images of the cornea and adjacent conjunctiva.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Reserved for atypical lesions — rapid growth, leukoplakia, irregular vessels, unilateral non-classic location — to exclude OSSN, amyloid, lymphoma, or atypical infection.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed as a minor procedure under topical or sub-Tenon anesthesia. The lesion is removed in one piece and sent to pathology; results typically return within 5-10 days.

diagnosticTests.test: Tear film and meibomian gland evaluation
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies coexisting dry eye disease, which worsens postoperative healing and accelerates recurrence if left untreated.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Tear breakup time, Schirmer test, and meibography. The complete workup takes 10-15 minutes and is largely painless apart from minor lid expression.

treatmentOverview: Treatment depends on whether the pterygium is symptomatic and whether it threatens vision. Asymptomatic atrophic pterygia far from the visual axis are observed with photographs at 6-12 month intervals, supported by topical lubricants four to six times daily and rigorous UV protection. Inflammatory flares are managed with a short course of topical low-potency corticosteroids (loteprednol or fluorometholone) for 1-2 weeks, sometimes preceded by a few days of topical NSAIDs; topical cyclosporine 0.05% is used for chronic inflammatory pterygium in patients with concurrent dry eye. Surgery is indicated when the lesion induces clinically significant astigmatism (over 1.0 diopter), approaches within 3 mm of the visual axis, causes chronic irritation refractory to medical therapy, restricts eye movement, or causes cosmetic distress that does not improve with medical care. The Kenyon conjunctival autograft, described in 1985, remains the gold standard: the pterygium head is dissected from the cornea, the body excised, and a free conjunctival graft from the superotemporal bulbar conjunctiva of the same eye is sutured or glued to cover the bare sclera. Recurrence with this technique is 2-15% in experienced hands. Limbal-conjunctival autograft includes a strip of limbal stem cells and lowers recurrence further in high-risk eyes. Amniotic membrane transplantation is an alternative when healthy donor conjunctiva is limited, with recurrence rates of roughly 10-25%. Fibrin glue versus sutures gives faster healing and less inflammation with equivalent recurrence (Cochrane 2016). Adjunctive mitomycin C 0.02% applied for 3-5 minutes intraoperatively reduces recurrence in high-risk cases but carries a small risk of scleral melt and is avoided in fragile or radiation-exposed eyes. The historical bare sclera technique is no longer recommended — recurrence rates of 30-90% make it inferior to all modern approaches. Postoperative care includes a topical antibiotic for one week, a tapering topical steroid for 4-6 weeks, intensive lubrication, and lifelong UV protection with wraparound polarized sunglasses and brimmed hats.

medicalTreatments.name: Preservative-free artificial tears (4-6 times daily)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line therapy for symptomatic but non-vision-threatening pterygium. Stabilizes the tear film, reduces friction over the elevated lesion, and quiets the chronic micro-inflammation that drives growth.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom control in roughly 70% of patients with atrophic or quiet intermediate lesions; does not regress the lesion but slows growth.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical low-potency corticosteroids (loteprednol 0.5% or fluorometholone 0.1%)
medicalTreatments.description: Short courses of 1-2 weeks for inflammatory flares with hyperemia and discomfort. Tapered rather than stopped abruptly. Monitor intraocular pressure and avoid prolonged courses to limit steroid-induced glaucoma and cataract risk.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Rapid reduction in redness, swelling, and discomfort in 80-90% of flares; recurrence of inflammation common without addressing UV and dry eye triggers.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical cyclosporine 0.05% twice daily
medicalTreatments.description: Used for chronic inflammatory pterygium and for patients with concurrent moderate-to-severe dry eye. Modulates ocular surface T-cell inflammation without the side effects of long-term steroids.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improvement in chronic inflammatory symptoms in 60-70% of patients over 3-6 months; useful adjunct before and after surgery.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical antihistamines / mast-cell stabilizers (olopatadine, ketotifen)
medicalTreatments.description: Indicated when pterygium symptoms coexist with allergic conjunctivitis. Reduce the allergic component that often amplifies foreign body sensation and lid rubbing.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom relief in 70-80% of patients with documented allergic comorbidity; no effect on pterygium growth itself.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical NSAIDs (ketorolac 0.5%, bromfenac 0.09%)
medicalTreatments.description: Short-term option for inflammatory flares in patients in whom topical steroids are contraindicated, such as those with steroid-induced ocular hypertension.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective short-term anti-inflammatory in roughly 60% of flares; risk of corneal epithelial toxicity and rare corneal melt with prolonged use.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical mitomycin C 0.02% (perioperative)
medicalTreatments.description: Applied intraoperatively for 3-5 minutes to the bare sclera in high-risk or recurrent pterygium to reduce fibroblast proliferation. Not used as a standalone medical therapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces recurrence by roughly 50% versus surgery alone in high-risk eyes; rare but serious risk of scleral melt and limbal ischemia.

surgicalOptions.name: Conjunctival autograft (Kenyon technique)
surgicalOptions.description: Gold-standard primary surgery. The pterygium head is dissected from the cornea using a crescent blade, the body excised, and a free graft of healthy bulbar conjunctiva from the superotemporal quadrant of the same eye is sutured or glued to the bare sclera. Performed under topical or sub-Tenon anesthesia in 30-45 minutes.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Recurrence 2-15% at 12 months in experienced hands; symptomatic relief in 90% of patients.

surgicalOptions.name: Limbal-conjunctival autograft
surgicalOptions.description: Modification of the Kenyon technique that includes a strip of limbal stem cells in the graft. Reinforces the limbal barrier in high-risk and recurrent cases.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Recurrence 1-8% at 12 months; preferred for fleshy T3 and recurrent pterygia.

surgicalOptions.name: Amniotic membrane transplantation
surgicalOptions.description: Used when donor conjunctiva is scarred or limited, in double pterygium, and in complex recurrent disease. The bare sclera is covered with cryopreserved or dehydrated amniotic membrane secured with sutures or fibrin glue.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Recurrence 10-25%; somewhat higher than autograft but useful when autograft is not feasible.

surgicalOptions.name: Conjunctival autograft with fibrin glue (vs sutures)
surgicalOptions.description: Tissue adhesive replaces 10-0 nylon or polyglactin sutures for fixing the graft. Operating time is shorter, patient comfort is better, and inflammatory response is reduced.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Equivalent recurrence to suture-fixed autograft (2-15%) with faster visual recovery (Cochrane 2016).

surgicalOptions.name: Adjunctive mitomycin C 0.02% for 3-5 minutes
surgicalOptions.description: Antimetabolite applied intraoperatively in high-risk, double, or recurrent pterygium. Reduces fibroblast proliferation and recurrence; requires meticulous irrigation and avoidance in fragile sclera.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Halves recurrence in high-risk eyes versus surgery alone; complication rate (scleral melt, calcific plaques) approximately 1-3%.

surgicalOptions.name: Bare sclera technique (historical, NOT recommended)
surgicalOptions.description: Simple excision leaving the underlying sclera uncovered. Once standard, now considered obsolete because of unacceptably high recurrence.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Recurrence 30-90%; reserved only for resource-limited settings with no alternative.

alternativeTreatments.name: Wraparound polarized sunglasses with UV400 protection
alternativeTreatments.description: Block roughly 96% of UV-B reaching the cornea and conjunctiva from front and side angles. The single most effective preventive intervention and a non-negotiable part of postoperative care to reduce recurrence.

alternativeTreatments.name: Wide-brimmed hats and visors
alternativeTreatments.description: Reduce direct overhead UV exposure by 50-60% when combined with sunglasses. Particularly useful for outdoor workers and surfers between sessions.

alternativeTreatments.name: Lid hygiene and warm compresses for meibomian gland dysfunction
alternativeTreatments.description: Daily warm compresses and lid scrubs improve tear film quality and reduce inflammatory flares of pterygium and the dry eye that frequently coexists.

alternativeTreatments.name: Omega-3 fatty acid supplementation (1-2 g daily EPA/DHA)
alternativeTreatments.description: Modest improvement in tear film stability and ocular surface inflammation in randomized trials; reasonable adjunct in patients with coexisting dry eye disease.

whySeeSpecialist: A cornea-trained ophthalmologist should be involved when the pterygium has reached or threatens the visual axis (within 3 mm), induces over 1.0 diopter of astigmatism, has failed medical therapy, has recurred after previous surgery, involves both nasal and temporal quadrants, or shows atypical features (rapid growth, leukoplakia, atypical vessels) suspicious for OSSN. Primary care optometrists and general ophthalmologists manage stable, atrophic, asymptomatic pterygia with lubricants and UV protection and refer when surgery becomes indicated.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for an ophthalmologist with cornea and anterior segment fellowship training, documented experience performing conjunctival autograft with low published recurrence rates (under 10%), comfort with limbal-conjunctival autograft and amniotic membrane techniques for recurrent disease, and a clinic equipped with corneal topography and anterior segment OCT. For recurrent or complex pterygium, choose a surgeon who routinely uses adjunctive mitomycin C and amniotic membrane and operates at a center with on-site pathology for atypical specimens.
preventionStrategies: Wear wraparound polarized sunglasses with UV400 protection any time you are outdoors, starting in childhood — this reduces corneal UV-B exposure by approximately 96%
Add a wide-brimmed hat or visor when working or playing outdoors; the combination with sunglasses cuts ocular UV dose by 70-80% versus no protection
Use protective eyewear in dusty, sandy, or windy environments to limit micro-trauma to the conjunctiva
Treat coexisting dry eye and meibomian gland dysfunction proactively — chronic inflammation amplifies UV-driven proliferation
Schedule annual eye exams if you live within 37 degrees of the equator, work outdoors, or have a family history of pterygium, so that early lesions are documented and tracked
lifestyleModifications: Make UV-blocking sunglasses a permanent habit, including on overcast days — up to 80% of UV penetrates clouds
Avoid prolonged unprotected exposure between 10 am and 4 pm when UV index peaks
Use polarized lenses on water, snow, and white sand where reflected UV is highest
Maintain a regular schedule of preservative-free artificial tears 4-6 times daily if you have established pterygium
Stop smoking — it worsens dry eye, postoperative inflammation, and graft healing
Avoid rubbing the eyes; chronic mechanical trauma accelerates conjunctival proliferation

recommended: Oily fish (salmon, sardines, mackerel) 2-3 times weekly for omega-3 fatty acids that support tear film quality
Leafy greens and orange/yellow vegetables for lutein and zeaxanthin, which accumulate in ocular tissues and may filter blue and UV light
Citrus fruits, peppers, and berries for vitamin C, an antioxidant in the cornea and aqueous humor
Nuts and seeds for vitamin E and zinc, both important for ocular surface health
Adequate water intake (1.5-2 liters daily) to support tear production
avoid: Excessive alcohol, which worsens dry eye and inflammation
Highly processed and ultra-refined foods that promote systemic inflammation
Smoking and exposure to second-hand smoke
Prolonged screen use without regular blinking breaks (the 20-20-20 rule) — does not cause pterygium but worsens symptoms

exerciseGuidelines: Regular exercise has no direct effect on pterygium but is generally beneficial. When exercising outdoors, wear wraparound polarized sunglasses and a brimmed hat. Avoid swimming in chlorinated pools without protective goggles in the first 4 weeks after pterygium surgery. Open-water swimming, surfing, and snow sports demand maximal UV protection because reflected UV roughly doubles the effective dose to the eye.
dailyManagement: Use preservative-free lubricating drops 4-6 times daily, more often in dry or windy environments
Wear wraparound UV400 sunglasses whenever outdoors, including overcast days
Photograph the lesion every 6-12 months on a phone using a consistent angle and lighting to track personal progression
Schedule an ophthalmology review every 6-12 months if stable, or sooner if vision changes, redness flares, or growth accelerates
Carry a short course of prescribed anti-inflammatory drops at home for early treatment of recognizable flares
Maintain lid hygiene with warm compresses and gentle lid scrubs if you have meibomian gland dysfunction
prognosis: Long-term prognosis is excellent for most patients. Atrophic, stable pterygia far from the visual axis often remain quiet for decades with simple lubrication and UV protection. After modern conjunctival autograft surgery in experienced hands, recurrence rates are 2-15% at 12 months, and the great majority of patients enjoy permanent symptomatic relief and normalization of astigmatism. Recurrent pterygium is more aggressive but can usually be controlled with limbal autograft, amniotic membrane, and adjunctive mitomycin C. The biggest determinants of long-term outcome are (1) consistent lifelong UV protection — recurrence after surgery rises sharply in patients who return to unprotected outdoor exposure — and (2) treatment of coexisting dry eye, which both accelerates growth and impairs graft healing. Pterygium does not progress to invasive cancer, but the differential with OSSN must be respected in atypical lesions. Visual prognosis depends on how close the lesion approached the visual axis before surgery; lesions removed before encroaching the optical zone usually leave the cornea fully clear and astigmatism-free.
recoveryTimeline: Topical lubricants and short courses of anti-inflammatory drops typically settle symptoms within 1-2 weeks. After conjunctival autograft surgery, the operated eye is red and uncomfortable for 5-7 days; foreign body sensation peaks at 24-72 hours. Most patients return to office work within a week and to outdoor work with full UV protection within 2-3 weeks. Sutures, when used, are removed or dissolve over 2-4 weeks. Visual acuity stabilizes by 4-6 weeks once corneal epithelium remodels and induced astigmatism resolves. Recurrence, if it occurs, almost always develops within the first 6-12 months postoperatively; surgeons schedule follow-up at 1 week, 1 month, 3 months, 6 months, and 12 months specifically to catch early recurrence when adjunctive treatment is still simple.
complications: Postoperative recurrence of the pterygium, more aggressive than the primary lesion in 30-50% of recurrent cases
Symblepharon (adhesion between the lid conjunctiva and globe) restricting eye movement, more common after bare sclera technique and large excisions
Persistent astigmatism or corneal scarring when the lesion has been allowed to encroach on the visual axis before surgery
Scleral melt or thinning, particularly after mitomycin C use in fragile or radiation-exposed eyes (rare, roughly 1-3%)
Graft dehiscence, retraction, or epithelial inclusion cysts after autograft (5-10%)
Steroid-induced ocular hypertension or cataract with prolonged or unsupervised topical steroid use
Rarely, infectious scleritis or endophthalmitis after surgery (under 1%)

supportResources.name: American Academy of Ophthalmology — Pterygium
supportResources.description: Authoritative US patient education from the national professional body for ophthalmology.

supportResources.name: National Eye Institute — Pterygium
supportResources.description: Plain-language overview from the US National Institutes of Health, including prevention and treatment options.

supportResources.name: Royal College of Ophthalmologists — Patient Information Leaflets
supportResources.description: UK clinical body resources including post-surgery information for conjunctival and corneal procedures.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cataract
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cataract is opacification of the lens behind the iris and causes painless gradual blurring without any visible surface growth. Pterygium is an external fleshy lesion on the conjunctiva and cornea; the lens is clear unless cataract coexists, which is common in the same older outdoor-exposed population.

confusedWithConditions.name: Glaucoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Glaucoma is optic neuropathy driven by elevated intraocular pressure and causes silent peripheral vision loss with no external eye changes. Pterygium causes a visible conjunctival lesion, redness, and astigmatism but does not affect optic nerve function or intraocular pressure.

confusedWithConditions.name: Allergic Conjunctivitis (allergic eye disease)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Allergic conjunctivitis produces bilateral itching, tearing, and diffuse redness with papillary reaction, typically seasonal and without a fixed fibrovascular mass. Pterygium produces a discrete, slowly enlarging triangular lesion that crosses the limbus and persists year-round.

confusedWithConditions.name: Ocular Surface Squamous Neoplasia (OSSN) — skin cancer differential
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: OSSN is a UV-related neoplasm of the conjunctival/corneal epithelium that can mimic an inflamed pterygium. Suspicious features include rapid growth, leukoplakia (white keratin), atypical feeder vessels, and irregular surface. OSSN requires biopsy, complete excision with margins, and topical chemotherapy — not standard pterygium surgery.

relatedConditions.name: Amyloid Pterygium
relatedConditions.relevance: Variant in which amyloid is deposited within the pterygium stroma; clinically may appear yellowish and waxy. Diagnosis confirmed on histopathology with Congo red staining.

relatedConditions.name: Central Pterygium
relatedConditions.relevance: Pterygium that has advanced toward or onto the central cornea, threatening or affecting the visual axis. Surgical removal is urgent to limit permanent visual loss.

relatedConditions.name: Double Pterygium
relatedConditions.relevance: Pterygia present on both nasal and temporal sides of the same eye, typically in heavily UV-exposed patients. Often requires staged surgery with amniotic membrane.

relatedConditions.name: Recurrent Pterygium
relatedConditions.relevance: Regrowth after previous excision, usually within 12 months. More aggressive than primary disease and managed with limbal autograft, amniotic membrane, and adjunctive mitomycin C.

relatedConditions.name: Cataract
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares UV-related risk profile and frequently coexists in older outdoor-exposed adults; the two conditions are often addressed sequentially or in combined surgery.

relatedConditions.name: Sjogren Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Severe dry eye associated with Sjogren accelerates pterygium growth and worsens postoperative healing; identifying and treating it improves surgical outcomes.

relatedConditions.name: Basal Cell Carcinoma (UV-related skin cancer)
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares chronic UV exposure as a primary driver; patients with pterygium are at elevated risk of skin cancers in the periorbital region and benefit from dermatology screening.

faqs.question: What is pterygium of the eye?
faqs.answer: Pterygium is a wing-shaped fibrovascular growth of the conjunctiva that crosses the limbus and onto the cornea, usually from the nasal side. It is caused mainly by years of UV exposure and is commonly known as Surfer's Eye. Most grow slowly; some need surgical removal once they affect vision.

faqs.question: Why is pterygium called Surfer's Eye?
faqs.answer: The nickname reflects the strong link between pterygium and chronic outdoor UV exposure, especially over water where UV reflects and intensifies the dose to the eye. Surfers, fishermen, farmers, and construction workers in tropical climates have the highest rates.

faqs.question: Is pterygium dangerous or cancerous?
faqs.answer: Pterygium is a benign degenerative growth and does not turn into cancer. The main risks are induced astigmatism, blurred vision near the pupil, and chronic irritation. The lookalike ocular surface squamous neoplasia (OSSN) is a true eye-surface cancer and needs biopsy if features are atypical.

faqs.question: What causes pterygium?
faqs.answer: The dominant cause is chronic UV-B exposure, particularly in tropical and equatorial climates. Dust, wind, dry environments, outdoor work, and genetic predisposition add risk. The Coroneo hypothesis explains the nasal pattern: peripheral light is focused by the cornea onto the nasal limbus.

faqs.question: Can pterygium go away without surgery?
faqs.answer: Pterygium does not regress once formed, but it can stay quiet for years with lubricant drops, anti-inflammatory therapy for flares, and consistent UV protection. Surgery is reserved for lesions that threaten vision, induce astigmatism, cause persistent discomfort, or are cosmetically disabling.

faqs.question: When does pterygium need surgery?
faqs.answer: Surgery is indicated when pterygium induces over 1 diopter of astigmatism, comes within 3 mm of the visual axis, causes irritation refractory to medical therapy, restricts eye movement, or is cosmetically disabling. Earlier surgery preserves corneal clarity and refractive outcome.

faqs.question: What is the best surgery for pterygium?
faqs.answer: Conjunctival autograft (Kenyon 1985) is the gold standard. The pterygium is removed and a healthy conjunctival graft from the same eye is placed over the bare sclera with sutures or fibrin glue. Recurrence is 2-15%. Bare sclera excision is obsolete because recurrence reaches 30-90%.

faqs.question: How long does pterygium surgery take and is it painful?
faqs.answer: The procedure takes 30-45 minutes per eye under topical or sub-Tenon anesthesia. Patients feel pressure but not sharp pain. Afterward, the eye is gritty, red, and uncomfortable for 5-7 days, with foreign body sensation peaking in 24-72 hours. Oral analgesics and lubricants manage discomfort well.

faqs.question: What is the recurrence rate after pterygium surgery?
faqs.answer: With modern conjunctival autograft, recurrence is 2-15% at 12 months. Adjunctive limbal stem cell transfer and mitomycin C 0.02% reduce recurrence in high-risk eyes. Bare sclera surgery (30-90% recurrence) is obsolete. Most recurrences occur within 6-12 months postoperatively.

faqs.question: How do I prevent pterygium recurrence after surgery?
faqs.answer: Lifelong wraparound UV400 sunglasses, brimmed hats, treatment of any coexisting dry eye, and continued lubrication for several months postoperatively are the most effective measures. Returning to unprotected outdoor exposure soon after surgery is the single biggest cause of recurrence.

faqs.question: What is the difference between pterygium and pinguecula?
faqs.answer: Pinguecula is a yellowish bump on the conjunctiva that stays on the white of the eye and does not cross the limbus. Pterygium is a triangular fibrovascular lesion that crosses the limbus and grows onto the cornea. Both share UV exposure as a cause.

faqs.question: Can children get pterygium?
faqs.answer: Pediatric pterygium is uncommon. When it occurs, it raises suspicion for xeroderma pigmentosum or another DNA repair disorder, especially if the child has freckling and sun sensitivity. Most pterygia first appear in adults aged 20-50 after years of UV accumulation.

faqs.question: Does pterygium affect vision?
faqs.answer: Small atrophic pterygia far from the pupil usually do not affect vision. As the lesion grows, it induces with-the-rule astigmatism, typically 0.5-3 diopters. Once the head approaches within 3 mm of the visual axis, irregular astigmatism and direct obstruction of light blur vision significantly.

faqs.question: Will my astigmatism improve after pterygium removal?
faqs.answer: In most cases, yes. Studies show induced astigmatism reduces by 50-90% within 4-6 weeks of conjunctival autograft surgery, with the largest improvement in lesions that caused over 1 diopter of cylinder. Any permanent corneal scar from a long-standing lesion may leave some residual astigmatism.

faqs.question: Are sunglasses really enough to prevent pterygium?
faqs.answer: Wraparound polarized sunglasses with UV400 protection block roughly 96% of UV-B reaching the cornea and are the single most effective preventive measure. Combined with a brimmed hat, they cut effective ocular UV dose by 70-80% versus no protection. Childhood and adolescent protection matters most.

faqs.question: Is pterygium hereditary?
faqs.answer: There is a genetic component. First-degree relatives of patients have roughly 2-3 times higher risk after accounting for shared environment. p53 mutations and certain matrix metalloproteinase variants have been linked, suggesting shared UV-induced mutational mechanisms with skin neoplasia.

faqs.question: What does pterygium look like?
faqs.answer: Pterygium appears as a pink or red triangular fleshy growth on the white of the eye, usually starting from the inner corner and pointing toward the pupil. As it grows it crosses the limbus onto the cornea and becomes more vascular and elevated over months to years.

faqs.question: How fast does pterygium grow?
faqs.answer: Growth is usually slow, over months to years. A typical lesion may take 5-10 years to reach the visual axis. Rapid growth over weeks or months is unusual and should prompt biopsy to exclude ocular surface squamous neoplasia, amyloid, or other atypical conditions.

faqs.question: Can I wear contact lenses with pterygium?
faqs.answer: Small atrophic pterygia are usually compatible with soft contact lenses, although foreign body sensation may worsen. Fleshy or elevated lesions interfere with lens fit and are irritated by lens edges. Scleral lenses are sometimes used after surgery for residual irregular astigmatism.

faqs.question: How much does pterygium surgery cost?
faqs.answer: Conjunctival autograft surgery typically costs 1,200-3,500 USD per eye in private US practice and 500-1,500 GBP privately in the UK. It is often covered by insurance or public systems when visually or symptomatically indicated. Adjunctive amniotic membrane or mitomycin C adds cost.

faqs.question: What should I do after pterygium surgery to protect my eye?
faqs.answer: Use prescribed antibiotic and steroid drops on schedule, lubricate frequently with preservative-free tears, wear wraparound UV400 sunglasses outdoors, avoid swimming and contact lenses for 4 weeks, and attend follow-ups at 1 week, 1 and 3 months, and 6 and 12 months.

faqs.question: Can pterygium come back in the other eye?
faqs.answer: Yes. UV exposure affects both eyes, so patients with pterygium in one eye are at higher risk of developing it in the other, especially if outdoor exposure continues without protection. Bilateral pterygium occurs in roughly 15-30% of patients in long-term follow-up.

metaDescription: Pterygium of eye: causes, symptoms, surgery, and recurrence of the UV-driven Surfer's Eye growth. Treatment and prevention reviewed by our doctors.

images.caption: Typical nasal pterygium — a triangular fibrovascular growth that has crossed the limbus onto the cornea.
